explanation.pearl: A well-conducted Delphi study producing a standardised outcome or variable definition is a highly citable contribution to the field — it enables future meta-analyses by providing a common language across studies.

questions.stem: According to ICMJE authorship criteria, a consultant surgeon who recruited patients into a trial, provided the study idea, and performed all operations but did NOT contribute to writing or critically revising the manuscript, and did NOT approve the final version, should be:

options.A: Listed as first author because they performed all operations
options.B: Listed as last author because they are the senior investigator
options.C: Listed in the Acknowledgements section, not as an author, because they do not meet all ICMJE criteria
options.D: Listed as corresponding author to ensure their contact details are publicly available

explanation.correct: ICMJE requires ALL four criteria to be met for authorship: (1) substantial contribution to conception/design OR acquisition/analysis/interpretation of data; (2) drafting OR critically revising the work; (3) final approval of the version to be published; (4) accountability for all aspects. This surgeon meets criteria 1 (operations, study concept) but fails criteria 2 (no manuscript contribution), 3 (no final approval), and 4 (accountability). They should be listed in the Acknowledgements as having provided surgical care and study leadership, but cannot be an author. Granting authorship without meeting all four criteria is gift authorship — an ICMJE and COPE violation.

wrong.A: Performing operations — while a crucial contribution — satisfies only criterion 1. Without manuscript contribution and final approval, first authorship is not merited.
wrong.B: Senior position or PI status does not automatically confer authorship. Authorship is earned by intellectual contribution to the work, not by hierarchy.
wrong.D: Corresponding author status conveys responsibility for the accuracy of all data — this cannot be assigned to someone who did not contribute to or approve the manuscript.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE Recommendations for the Conduct, Reporting, Editing, and Publication of Scholarly Work in Medical Journals — authorship criteria (2023 update)
explanation.trial: McNutt M et al. (Science 2018): analysis of authorship practices — gift and ghost authorship remain prevalent in surgical journals despite ICMJE guidance.
explanation.pearl: Before submitting any manuscript, verify that every listed author genuinely satisfies all four ICMJE criteria. As first author, you are responsible for ensuring this — undisclosed gift authorship discovered post-publication reflects on all named authors.

questions.stem: A surgical researcher submits an observational study using NHS hospital episode statistics (HES) data to a journal. Which open science practice is MOST applicable and increasingly expected by journals for this type of study?

options.A: Publishing a protocol paper before the study begins and sharing the anonymised analysis dataset and statistical code alongside the manuscript
options.B: Pre-registering the study on ClinicalTrials.gov (mandatory for all observational studies)
options.C: Making the HES data itself publicly available via a free download repository
options.D: Open science practices only apply to RCTs — observational studies are exempt

explanation.correct: For observational studies using routine data, the key open science practices are: (1) pre-registration of the study protocol and statistical analysis plan on OSF or ISRCTN before data analysis begins (not mandatory but increasingly expected); (2) publishing a protocol paper describing the study design, exposure and outcome definitions, and statistical approach; (3) sharing the analysis code (R/Stata/SAS scripts) so others can verify the analysis; (4) providing a data access statement explaining how the underlying data can be accessed (HES data cannot be freely shared but the access pathway via NHS Digital/UKRI can be described). Sharing the analysis code enables reproducibility even when the raw data cannot be shared.

wrong.A: This is substantially correct — protocol registration plus code sharing is the current best practice for observational studies using routinely collected data.
wrong.B: ClinicalTrials.gov is primarily for interventional studies (clinical trials). Observational studies are better registered on OSF, ISRCTN, or the WHO registry for observational studies.
wrong.C: HES data are NHS patient data containing sensitive personal health information — they cannot be made publicly available. Access is through NHS Digital Data Access Request Committee with appropriate governance.
wrong.D: Open science practices increasingly apply to all study types, including observational. The STROBE guidelines companion paper (STROBE-STARD) and RECORD statement explicitly recommend open practices for observational studies.

explanation.guideline: RECORD Statement 2015 — reporting standards for observational studies using routinely collected data; OSBORNE open science framework for observational research
explanation.trial: Hippisley-Cox J et al. (BMJ QResearch studies) — routinely publish Stata analysis code alongside papers using CPRD/HES data, enabling independent replication.
explanation.pearl: Even when you cannot share the raw data (due to governance restrictions), sharing the analysis code and a synthetic data example is a meaningful contribution to reproducibility.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate first step for a PGY3 surgical resident who suspects that a senior colleague has fabricated operative time data in a published case series?

options.A: Immediately contact the publishing journal's editor directly with the allegation
options.B: Raise concerns with the institutional Research Integrity Officer or follow the local research misconduct procedure, as most institutions have a formal process for this
options.C: Publish a letter to the editor criticising the data without notifying the institution
options.D: Ignore the concern — junior trainees should not challenge senior colleagues on research matters

explanation.correct: Suspected research misconduct should be raised through formal institutional channels in the first instance. Most UK NHS Trusts and universities have a Research Integrity Officer or designated person for this purpose, following the UKRIO Code of Practice for Research. The institutional investigation can: access original source data; interview witnesses; examine laboratory notebooks and electronic records; involve an independent external reviewer. The institution notifies the journal, funder, and GMC if the investigation confirms misconduct. Going to the journal directly first may be appropriate if the institution itself is implicated, but standard first step is institutional reporting.

wrong.A: Direct journal contact bypasses the institutional investigation process that has access to original data and witnesses. Journals typically ask whether institutional investigation has been initiated before acting.
wrong.C: Publishing an allegation without investigation exposes the trainee to defamation risk and does not follow due process. COPE guidance recommends institutional investigation before public allegations.
wrong.D: All researchers — regardless of seniority — have both the right and the professional obligation to raise genuine concerns about research integrity. The GMC Good Medical Practice requires honesty in research. Hierarchy does not override this duty.

explanation.guideline: UKRIO Code of Practice for Research 2019 — institutional responsibilities for investigation; GMC Good Medical Practice 2013 Para 71 — honesty in research reporting
explanation.trial: Stapel affair (psychology, 2011) — case study in research misconduct investigation process: initial concern raised by junior researchers to institutional committee led to full investigation and >50 retractions.
explanation.pearl: Whistleblowing in research is protected in the UK under the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998. You cannot be legitimately dismissed or penalised for raising a genuine concern about research misconduct through proper channels.
